narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and LSAT score of 24, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 96% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in most applicants who get approved. This high predicted risk directly led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed on the bar based on patterns from thousands of prior students. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT performance, and your Year 1 school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—meaning you ranked in the bottom tenth of your school that year—had the strongest negative impact, strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure compared to peers who ranked higher. Similarly, your LSAT score pushed the prediction further toward failure, as it fell well short of the levels typically associated with strong bar outcomes. Even your Year 1 decile of 1 contributed in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance relative to the average student's mid-level standing. While factors like your full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority of successful students, they weren't enough to offset these core academic signals. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often have decile rankings around the middle of their classes and much higher LSATs, your metrics placed you in a riskier group based on historical bar passage data. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances have succeeded with targeted improvements—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to protect the school's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades in any remaining coursework to climb those deciles, and perhaps seeking mentorship or prep programs tailored for students from underrepresented backgrounds like yours. With focused effort on these areas, you could reapply stronger and shift the odds in your favor next time.
